NKJV.1: Then Agrippa said to Paul, “You are permitted to speak for yourself.” So Paul stretched out his hand and answered for himself:
NKJV.2: “I think myself happy, King Agrippa, because today I shall answer for myself before you concerning all the things of which I am accused by the Jews,
NKJV.3: especially because you are expert in all customs and questions which have to do with the Jews. Therefore I beg you to hear me patiently.
NKJV.4: “My manner of life from my youth, which was spent from the beginning among my own nation at Jerusalem, all the Jews know.
NKJV.5: They knew me from the first, if they were willing to testify, that according to the strictest sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee.
NKJV.6: And now I stand and am judged for the hope of the promise made by God to our fathers.
NKJV.7: To this promise our twelve tribes, earnestly serving God night and day, hope to attain. For this hope’s sake, King Agrippa, I am accused by the Jews.
NKJV.8: Why should it be thought incredible by you that God raises the dead?
NKJV.9: “Indeed, I myself thought I must do many things contrary to the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
NKJV.10: This I also did in Jerusalem, and many of the saints I shut up in prison, having received authority from the chief priests; and when they were put to death, I cast my vote against them.
NKJV.11: And I punished them often in every synagogue and compelled them to blaspheme; and being exceedingly enraged against them, I persecuted them even to foreign cities.
NKJV.12: “While thus occupied, as I journeyed to Damascus with authority and commission from the chief priests,
NKJV.13: at midday, O king, along the road I saw a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining around me and those who journeyed with me.
NKJV.14: And when we all had fallen to the ground, I heard a voice speaking to me and saying in the Hebrew language, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads.’
NKJV.15: So I said, ‘Who are You, Lord?’ And He said, ‘I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.
NKJV.16: But rise and stand on your feet; for I have appeared to you for this purpose,  to make you a minister and a witness both of the things which you have seen and of the things which I will yet reveal to you.
NKJV.17: I will deliver you from the Jewish people, as well as from the Gentiles, to whom I now send you,
NKJV.18: to open their eyes, in order to turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and an inheritance among those who are sanctified by faith in Me.’
NKJV.19: “Therefore, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision,
NKJV.20: but declared first to those in Damascus and in Jerusalem, and throughout all the region of Judea, and then to the Gentiles, that they should repent, turn to God, and do works befitting repentance.
NKJV.21: For these reasons the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me.
NKJV.22: Therefore, having obtained help from God, to this day I stand, witnessing both to small and great, saying no other things than those which the prophets and Moses said would come—
NKJV.23: that the Christ would suffer, that He would be the first to rise from the dead, and would proclaim light to the Jewish people and to the Gentiles.”
NKJV.24: Now as he thus made his defense, Festus said with a loud voice, “Paul, you are beside yourself! Much learning is driving you mad!”
NKJV.25: But he said, “I am not mad, most noble Festus, but speak the words of truth and reason.
NKJV.26: For the king, before whom I also speak freely, knows these things; for I am convinced that none of these things escapes his attention, since this thing was not done in a corner.
NKJV.27: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you do believe.”
NKJV.28: Then Agrippa said to Paul, “You almost persuade me to become a Christian.”
NKJV.29: And Paul said, “I would to God that not only you, but also all who hear me today, might become both almost and altogether such as I am, except for these chains.”
NKJV.30: When he had said these things, the king stood up, as well as the governor and Bernice and those who sat with them;
NKJV.31: and when they had gone aside, they talked among themselves, saying, “This man is doing nothing deserving of death or chains.”
NKJV.32: Then Agrippa said to Festus, “This man might have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar.”

ESV.1: So Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.” Then Paul stretched out his hand and made his defense:
ESV.2: “I consider myself fortunate that it is before you, King Agrippa, I am going to make my defense today against all the accusations of the Jews,
ESV.3: especially because you are familiar with all the customs and controversies of the Jews. Therefore I beg you to listen to me patiently.
ESV.4: “My manner of life from my youth, spent from the beginning among my own nation and in Jerusalem, is known by all the Jews.
ESV.5: They have known for a long time, if they are willing to testify, that according to the strictest party of our religion I have lived as a Pharisee.
ESV.6: And now I stand here on trial because of my hope in the promise made by God to our fathers,
ESV.7: to which our twelve tribes hope to attain, as they earnestly worship night and day. And for this hope I am accused by Jews, O king!
ESV.8: Why is it thought incredible by any of you that God raises the dead?
ESV.9: “I myself was convinced that I ought to do many things in opposing the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
ESV.10: And I did so in Jerusalem. I not only locked up many of the saints in prison after receiving authority from the chief priests, but when they were put to death I cast my vote against them.
ESV.11: And I punished them often in all the synagogues and tried to make them blaspheme, and in raging fury against them I persecuted them even to foreign cities.
ESV.12: “In this connection I journeyed to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests.
ESV.13: At midday, O king, I saw on the way a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, that shone around me and those who journeyed with me.
ESV.14: And when we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language,  ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads.’
ESV.15: And I said, ‘Who are you, Lord?’ And the Lord said, ‘I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.
ESV.16: But rise and stand upon your feet, for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you as a servant and witness to the things in which you have seen me and to those in which I will appear to you,
ESV.17: delivering you from your people and from the Gentiles — to whom I am sending you
ESV.18: to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me.’
ESV.19: “Therefore, O King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision,
ESV.20: but declared first to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout all the region of Judea, and also to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, performing deeds in keeping with their repentance.
ESV.21: For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me.
ESV.22: To this day I have had the help that comes from God, and so I stand here testifying both to small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would come to pass:
ESV.23: that the Christ must suffer and that, by being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles.”
ESV.24: And as he was saying these things in his defense, Festus said with a loud voice, “Paul, you are out of your mind; your great learning is driving you out of your mind.”
ESV.25: But Paul said, “I am not out of my mind, most excellent Festus, but I am speaking true and rational words.
ESV.26: For the king knows about these things, and to him I speak boldly. For I am persuaded that none of these things has escaped his notice, for this has not been done in a corner.
ESV.27: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe.”
ESV.28: And Agrippa said to Paul, “In a short time would you persuade me to be a Christian?”
ESV.29: And Paul said, “Whether short or long, I would to God that not only you but also all who hear me this day might become such as I am — except for these chains.”
ESV.30: Then the king rose, and the governor and Bernice and those who were sitting with them.
ESV.31: And when they had withdrawn, they said to one another, “This man is doing nothing to deserve death or imprisonment.”
ESV.32: And Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar.”

NASB.1: Agrippa said to Paul, 'You are permitted to speak for yourself.' Then Paul stretched out his hand and [proceeded] to make his defense:
NASB.2: 'In regard to all the things of which I am accused by the Jews, I consider myself fortunate, King Agrippa, that I am about to make my defense before you today;
NASB.3: especially because you are an expert in all customs and questions among [the] Jews; therefore I beg you to listen to me patiently.
NASB.4: 'So then, all Jews know my manner of life from my youth up, which from the beginning was spent among my [own] nation and at Jerusalem;
NASB.5: since they have known about me for a long time, if they are willing to testify, that I lived [as] a Pharisee according to the strictest sect of our religion.
NASB.6: 'And now I am standing trial for the hope of the promise made by God to our fathers;
NASB.7: [the promise] to which our twelve tribes hope to attain, as they earnestly serve [God] night and day. And for this hope, O King, I am being accused by Jews.
NASB.8: 'Why is it considered incredible among you [people] if God does raise the dead?
NASB.9: 'So then, I thought to myself that I had to do many things hostile to the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
NASB.10: 'And this is just what I did in Jerusalem; not only did I lock up many of the saints in prisons, having received authority from the chief priests, but also when they were being put to death I cast my vote against them.
NASB.11: 'And as I punished them often in all the synagogues, I tried to force them to blaspheme; and being furiously enraged at them, I kept pursuing them even to foreign cities.
NASB.12: 'While so engaged as I was journeying to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests,
NASB.13: at midday, O King, I saw on the way a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining all around me and those who were journeying with me.
NASB.14: 'And when we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew dialect, 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads.'
NASB.15: 'And I said, 'Who are You, Lord?' And the Lord said, 'I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.
NASB.16: 'But get up and stand on your feet; for this purpose I have appeared to you, to appoint you a minister and a witness not only to the things which you have seen, but also to the things in which I will appear to you;
NASB.17: rescuing you from the [Jewish] people and from the Gentiles, to whom I am sending you,
NASB.18: to open their eyes so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the dominion of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and an inheritance among those who have been sanctified by faith in Me.'
NASB.19: 'So, King Agrippa, I did not prove disobedient to the heavenly vision,
NASB.20: but [kept] declaring both to those of Damascus first, and [also] at Jerusalem and [then] throughout all the region of Judea, and [even] to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, performing deeds appropriate to repentance.
NASB.21: 'For this reason [some] Jews seized me in the temple and tried to put me to death.
NASB.22: 'So, having obtained help from God, I stand to this day testifying both to small and great, stating nothing but what the Prophets and Moses said was going to take place;
NASB.23: that the Christ was to suffer, [and] that by reason of [His] resurrection from the dead He would be the first to proclaim light both to the [Jewish] people and to the Gentiles.'
NASB.24: While [Paul] was saying this in his defense, Festus said in a loud voice, 'Paul, you are out of your mind! [Your] great learning is driving you mad.'
NASB.25: But Paul said, 'I am not out of my mind, most excellent Festus, but I utter words of sober truth.
NASB.26: 'For the king knows about these matters, and I speak to him also with confidence, since I am persuaded that none of these things escape his notice; for this has not been done in a corner.
NASB.27: 'King Agrippa, do you believe the Prophets? I know that you do.'
NASB.28: Agrippa [replied] to Paul, 'In a short time you will persuade me to become a Christian.'
NASB.29: And Paul [said], 'I would wish to God, that whether in a short or long time, not only you, but also all who hear me this day, might become such as I am, except for these chains.'
NASB.30: The king stood up and the governor and Bernice, and those who were sitting with them,
NASB.31: and when they had gone aside, they [began] talking to one another, saying, 'This man is not doing anything worthy of death or imprisonment.'
NASB.32: And Agrippa said to Festus, 'This man might have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar.'

NLT.1: Then Agrippa said to Paul, “You may speak in your defense.” So Paul, gesturing with his hand, started his defense:
NLT.2: “I am fortunate, King Agrippa, that you are the one hearing my defense today against all these accusations made by the Jewish leaders,
NLT.3: for I know you are an expert on all Jewish customs and controversies. Now please listen to me patiently!
NLT.4: “As the Jewish leaders are well aware, I was given a thorough Jewish training from my earliest childhood among my own people and in Jerusalem.
NLT.5: If they would admit it, they know that I have been a member of the Pharisees, the strictest sect of our religion.
NLT.6: Now I am on trial because of my hope in the fulfillment of God’s promise made to our ancestors.
NLT.7: In fact, that is why the twelve tribes of Israel zealously worship God night and day, and they share the same hope I have. Yet, Your Majesty, they accuse me for having this hope!
NLT.8: Why does it seem incredible to any of you that God can raise the dead?
NLT.9: “I used to believe that I ought to do everything I could to oppose the very name of Jesus the Nazarene.
NLT.10: Indeed, I did just that in Jerusalem. Authorized by the leading priests, I caused many believers there to be sent to prison. And I cast my vote against them when they were condemned to death.
NLT.11: Many times I had them punished in the synagogues to get them to curse Jesus. I was so violently opposed to them that I even chased them down in foreign cities.
NLT.12: “One day I was on such a mission to Damascus, armed with the authority and commission of the leading priests.
NLT.13: About noon, Your Majesty, as I was on the road, a light from heaven brighter than the sun shone down on me and my companions.
NLT.14: We all fell down, and I heard a voice saying to me in Aramaic, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is useless for you to fight against my will. ’
NLT.15: “‘Who are you, lord?’ I asked. “And the Lord replied, ‘I am Jesus, the one you are persecuting.
NLT.16: Now get to your feet! For I have appeared to you to appoint you as my servant and witness. Tell people that you have seen me, and tell them what I will show you in the future.
NLT.17: And I will rescue you from both your own people and the Gentiles. Yes, I am sending you to the Gentiles
NLT.18: to open their eyes, so they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God. Then they will receive forgiveness for their sins and be given a place among God’s people, who are set apart by faith in me.’
NLT.19: “And so, King Agrippa, I obeyed that vision from heaven.
NLT.20: I preached first to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout all Judea, and also to the Gentiles, that all must repent of their sins and turn to God — and prove they have changed by the good things they do.
NLT.21: Some Jews arrested me in the Temple for preaching this, and they tried to kill me.
NLT.22: But God has protected me right up to this present time so I can testify to everyone, from the least to the greatest. I teach nothing except what the prophets and Moses said would happen —
NLT.23: that the Messiah would suffer and be the first to rise from the dead, and in this way announce God’s light to Jews and Gentiles alike.”
NLT.24: Suddenly, Festus shouted, “Paul, you are insane. Too much study has made you crazy!”
NLT.25: But Paul replied, “I am not insane, Most Excellent Festus. What I am saying is the sober truth.
NLT.26: And King Agrippa knows about these things. I speak boldly, for I am sure these events are all familiar to him, for they were not done in a corner!
NLT.27: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know you do — ”
NLT.28: Agrippa interrupted him. “Do you think you can persuade me to become a Christian so quickly?”
NLT.29: Paul replied, “Whether quickly or not, I pray to God that both you and everyone here in this audience might become the same as I am, except for these chains.”
NLT.30: Then the king, the governor, Bernice, and all the others stood and left.
NLT.31: As they went out, they talked it over and agreed, “This man hasn’t done anything to deserve death or imprisonment.”
NLT.32: And Agrippa said to Festus, “He could have been set free if he hadn’t appealed to Caesar.”

WEB.1: Agrippa said to Paul, “You may speak for yourself.” Then Paul stretched out his hand, and made his defense.
WEB.2: “I think myself happy, King Agrippa, that I am to make my defense before you today concerning all the things that I am accused by the Jews,
WEB.3: especially because you are expert in all customs and questions which are among the Jews. Therefore I beg you to hear me patiently.
WEB.4: “Indeed, all the Jews know my way of life from my youth up, which was from the beginning among my own nation and at Jerusalem;
WEB.5: having known me from the first, if they are willing to testify, that after the strictest sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee.
WEB.6: Now I stand here to be judged for the hope of the promise made by God to our fathers,
WEB.7: which our twelve tribes, earnestly serving night and day, hope to attain. Concerning this hope I am accused by the Jews, King Agrippa!
WEB.8: Why is it judged incredible with you, if God does raise the dead?
WEB.9: “I myself most certainly thought that I ought to do many things contrary to the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
WEB.10: This I also did in Jerusalem. I both shut up many of the saints in prisons, having received authority from the chief priests, and when they were put to death I gave my vote against them.
WEB.11: Punishing them often in all the synagogues, I tried to make them blaspheme. Being exceedingly enraged against them, I persecuted them even to foreign cities.
WEB.12: “Whereupon as I traveled to Damascus with the authority and commission from the chief priests,
WEB.13: at noon, O king, I saw on the way a light from the sky, brighter than the sun, shining around me and those who traveled with me.
WEB.14: When we had all fallen to the earth, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads.’
WEB.15: “I said, ‘Who are you, Lord?’ “He said, ‘I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.
WEB.16: But arise, and stand on your feet, for I have appeared to you for this purpose: to appoint you a servant and a witness both of the things which you have seen, and of the things which I will reveal to you;
WEB.17: delivering you from the people, and from the Gentiles, to whom I send you,
WEB.18: to open their eyes, that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive remission of sins and an inheritance among those who are sanctified by faith in me.’
WEB.19: “Therefore, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision,
WEB.20: but declared first to them of Damascus, at Jerusalem, and throughout all the country of Judea, and also to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, doing works worthy of repentance.
WEB.21: For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple, and tried to kill me.
WEB.22: Having therefore obtained the help that is from God, I stand to this day testifying both to small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would happen,
WEB.23: how the Christ must suffer, and how, by the resurrection of the dead, he would be first to proclaim light both to these people and to the Gentiles.”
WEB.24: As he thus made his defense, Festus said with a loud voice, “Paul, you are crazy! Your great learning is driving you insane!”
WEB.25: But he said, “I am not crazy, most excellent Festus, but boldly declare words of truth and reasonableness.
WEB.26: For the king knows of these things, to whom also I speak freely. For I am persuaded that none of these things is hidden from him, for this has not been done in a corner.
WEB.27: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe.”
WEB.28: Agrippa said to Paul, “With a little persuasion are you trying to make me a Christian?”
WEB.29: Paul said, “I pray to God, that whether with little or with much, not only you, but also all that hear me today, might become such as I am, except for these bonds.”
WEB.30: The king rose up with the governor, and Bernice, and those who sat with them.
WEB.31: When they had withdrawn, they spoke to one another, saying, “This man does nothing worthy of death or of bonds.”
WEB.32: Agrippa said to Festus, “This man might have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar.”

CSB17.1: Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.” Then Paul stretched out his hand and began his defense:
CSB17.2: “I consider myself fortunate, that it is before you, King Agrippa, I am to make my defense today against all the accusations of the Jews,
CSB17.3: especially since you are very knowledgeable about all the Jewish customs and controversies. Therefore I beg you to listen to me patiently.
CSB17.4: “All the Jews know my way of life from my youth, which was spent from the beginning among my own people and in Jerusalem. <sup>ⓒ</sup>
CSB17.5: They have known me for a long time, if they are willing to testify, that according to the strictest sect of our religion I lived as a Pharisee. <sup>ⓓ</sup>
CSB17.6: And now I stand on trial because of the hope <sup>ⓔ</sup> in what God promised <sup>ⓕ</sup> to our ancestors,
CSB17.7: the promise our twelve tribes hope to reach as they earnestly serve him night and day. King Agrippa, I am being accused by the Jews because of this hope. <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.8: Why do any of you consider it incredible that God raises the dead?
CSB17.9: In fact, I myself was convinced that it was necessary to do many things in opposition to the name of Jesus of Nazareth. <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.10: I actually did this in Jerusalem, and I locked up many of the saints in prison, since I had received authority for that from the chief priests. When they were put to death, I was in agreement against them. <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.11: In all the synagogues I often punished them and tried to make them blaspheme. <sup>ⓙ</sup> Since I was terribly enraged at them, I pursued them even to foreign cities.
CSB17.12: “I was traveling to Damascus under <sup>ⓚ</sup> these circumstances with authority and a commission from the chief priests.
CSB17.13: King Agrippa, while on the road at midday, I saw a light from heaven brighter than the sun, shining around me and those traveling with me.
CSB17.14: We all fell to the ground, and I heard a voice speaking to me in Aramaic, <sup>[1]</sup>‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads.’ <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.15: “I asked, ‘Who are you, Lord?’ “And the Lord replied: ‘I am Jesus, the one you are persecuting.
CSB17.16: But get up and stand on your feet. For I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you as a servant and a witness of what you have seen and will see of me. <sup>ⓜ</sup>
CSB17.17: I will rescue you from your people and from the Gentiles. I am sending you to them <sup>ⓝ</sup>
CSB17.18: to open their eyes <sup>ⓐ</sup>so that they may turn <sup>[2]</sup>from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a share among those who are sanctified by faith in me.’ <sup>ⓑ</sup>
CSB17.19: “So then, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision.
CSB17.20: Instead, I preached to those in Damascus first, and to those in Jerusalem and in all the region of Judea, and to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, and do works worthy of repentance. <sup>ⓒ</sup>
CSB17.21: For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple and were trying to kill me.
CSB17.22: To this very day, I have had help from God, and I stand and testify to both small and great, saying nothing other than what the prophets and Moses said would take place <sup>ⓓ</sup>  —
CSB17.23: that the Messiah must suffer, and that, as the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light to our people and to the Gentiles.” <sup>ⓔ</sup>
CSB17.24: As he was saying these things in his defense, Festus exclaimed in a loud voice, “You’re out of your mind, <sup>ⓕ</sup> Paul! Too much study is driving you mad.”
CSB17.25: But Paul replied, “I’m not out of my mind, most excellent Festus. On the contrary, I’m speaking words of truth and good judgment. <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.26: For the king knows about these matters, and I can speak boldly to him. For I am convinced that none of these things has escaped his notice, since this was not done in a corner.
CSB17.27: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know you believe.”
CSB17.28: Agrippa said to Paul, “Are you going to persuade me to become a Christian so easily?” <sup>[3]</sup>
CSB17.29: “I wish before God,” replied Paul, “that whether easily or with difficulty, <sup>[4]</sup> not only you but all who listen to me today might become as I am — except for these chains.” <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.30: The king, the governor, Bernice, and those sitting with them got up,
CSB17.31: and when they had left they talked with each other and said, “This man is not doing anything to deserve death or imprisonment.” <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.32: Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar.” <sup>ⓙ</sup>

AMP.1: Then Agrippa said to Paul, "You are [now] permitted to speak on your own behalf." At that, Paul stretched out his hand [as an orator] and made his defense [as follows]:
AMP.2: "I consider myself fortunate, King Agrippa, since it is before you that I am to make my defense today regarding all the charges brought against me by the Jews,
AMP.3: especially because you are an expert [fully knowledgeable, experienced and unusually conversant] in all the Jewish customs and controversial issues; therefore, I beg you to listen to me patiently.
AMP.4: "So then, all the Jews know my manner of life from my youth up, which from the beginning was spent among my own nation [the Jewish people], and in Jerusalem.
AMP.5: They have known me for a long time, if they are willing to testify to it, that according to the strictest sect of our religion, I have lived as a Pharisee.
AMP.6: And now I am standing trial for the hope of the promise made by God to our fathers.
AMP.7: Which hope [of the Messiah and the resurrection] our twelve tribes [confidently] expect to realize as they serve and worship God in earnest night and day. And for this hope, O King, I am being accused by Jews!
AMP.8: Why is it thought incredible by [any of] you that God raises the dead?
AMP.9: "So then, I [once] thought to myself that it was my duty to do many things in opposition to the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
AMP.10: And this is just what I did in Jerusalem; I not only locked up many of the saints (God's people) in prison after receiving authority from the chief priests, but also when they were being condemned to death, I cast my vote against them.
AMP.11: And I often punished them [making them suffer] in all the synagogues and tried to force them to blaspheme; and in my extreme rage at them, I kept hunting them even to foreign cities [harassing and persecuting them].
AMP.12: "While so engaged, as I was traveling to Damascus with the authority and commission and full power of the chief priests,
AMP.13: at midday, O King, I saw on the way a light from heaven surpassing the brightness of the sun, shining all around me and those who were traveling with me.
AMP.14: And when we all had fallen to the ground, I heard a voice in the Hebrew dialect (Jewish Aramaic) saying to me, 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me? It is hard for you to kick [repeatedly] against the goads [offering pointless resistance].'
AMP.15: And I said, 'Who are You, Lord?' And the Lord said, 'I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.
AMP.16: Get up and stand on your feet. I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you [to serve] as a minister and as a witness [to testify, with authority,] not only to the things which you have seen, but also to the things in which I will appear to you,
AMP.17: [choosing you for Myself and] rescuing you from the Jewish people and from the Gentiles, to whom I am sending you,
AMP.18: to open their [spiritual] eyes so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness and release from their sins and an inheritance among those who have been sanctified (set apart, made holy) by faith in Me.'
AMP.19: "So, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision,
AMP.20: but I openly proclaimed first to those at Damascus, then at Jerusalem and throughout the region of Judea, and even to the Gentiles, that they should repent [change their inner self—their old way of thinking] and turn to God, doing deeds and living lives which are consistent with repentance.
AMP.21: Because of this some Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me.
AMP.22: But I have had help from God to this day, and I stand [before people] testifying to small and great alike, stating nothing except what the Prophets and Moses said would come to pass—
AMP.23: that the Christ (the Messiah, the Anointed) was to suffer, and that He by being the first to rise from the dead [with an incorruptible body] would proclaim light (salvation) both to the Jewish people and to the Gentiles."
AMP.24: While Paul was making this defense, Festus said loudly, "Paul, you are out of your mind! Your great education is turning you toward madness."
AMP.25: But Paul replied, "I am not out of my mind, most excellent and noble Festus, but [with a sound mind] I am uttering rational words of truth and reason.
AMP.26: For [your majesty] the king understands these things, and [therefore] I am also speaking to him with confidence and boldness, since I am convinced that none of these things escape his notice; for this has not been done in a corner [hidden from view, in secret].
AMP.27: King Agrippa, do you believe the [writings of the] Prophets [their messages and words]? I know that you do."
AMP.28: Then Agrippa said to Paul, "In a short time [and with so little effort] you [almost] persuade me to become a Christian."
AMP.29: And Paul replied, "Whether in a short time or long, I wish to God that not only you, but also all who hear me today, might become such as I am, except for these chains."
AMP.30: Then the king stood up, and [with him] the governor and Bernice, and those who were sitting with them;
AMP.31: and after they had gone out, they began saying to one another, "This man is not doing anything worthy of death or [even] of imprisonment."
AMP.32: And Agrippa said to Festus, "This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar (Emperor Nero)."

MSG.1: Agrippa spoke directly to Paul: "Go ahead—tell us about yourself." Paul took the stand and told his story.
MSG.2: "I can't think of anyone, King Agrippa, before whom I'd rather be answering all these Jewish accusations than you,
MSG.3: knowing how well you are acquainted with Jewish ways and all our family quarrels.
MSG.4: "From the time of my youth, my life has been lived among my own people in Jerusalem.
MSG.5: Practically every Jew in town who watched me grow up—and if they were willing to stick their necks out they'd tell you in person—knows that I lived as a strict Pharisee, the most demanding branch of our religion.
MSG.6: It's because I believed it and took it seriously,
MSG.7: committed myself heart and soul to what God promised my ancestors—the identical hope, mind you, that the twelve tribes have lived for night and day all these centuries—it's because I have held on to this tested and tried hope that I'm being called on the carpet by the Jews. They should be the ones standing trial here, not me!
MSG.8: For the life of me, I can't see why it's a criminal offense to believe that God raises the dead.
MSG.9: "I admit that I didn't always hold to this position. For a time I thought it was my duty to oppose this Jesus of Nazareth with all my might.
MSG.10: Backed with the full authority of the high priests, I threw these believers—I had no idea they were God's people!—into the Jerusalem jail right and left, and whenever it came to a vote, I voted for their execution.
MSG.11: I stormed through their meeting places, bullying them into cursing Jesus, a one-man terror obsessed with obliterating these people. And then I started on the towns outside Jerusalem.
MSG.12: "One day on my way to Damascus, armed as always with papers from the high priests authorizing my action,
MSG.13: right in the middle of the day a blaze of light, light outshining the sun, poured out of the sky on me and my companions. Oh, King, it was so bright!
MSG.14: We fell flat on our faces. Then I heard a voice in Hebrew: 'Saul, Saul, why are you out to get me? Why do you insist on going against the grain?'
MSG.15: "I said, 'Who are you, Master?' "The voice answered, 'I am Jesus, the One you're hunting down like an animal.
MSG.16: But now, up on your feet—I have a job for you. I've handpicked you to be a servant and witness to what's happened today, and to what I am going to show you.
MSG.18: to open the eyes of the outsiders so they can see the difference between dark and light, and choose light, see the difference between Satan and God, and choose God. I'm sending you off to present my offer of sins forgiven, and a place in the family, inviting them into the company of those who begin real living by believing in me.'
MSG.19: "What could I do, King Agrippa? I couldn't just walk away from a vision like that! I became an obedient believer on the spot.
MSG.20: I started preaching this life-change—this radical turn to God and everything it meant in everyday life—right there in Damascus, went on to Jerusalem and the surrounding countryside, and from there to the whole world.
MSG.21: "It's because of this 'whole world' dimension that the Jews grabbed me in the Temple that day and tried to kill me. They want to keep God for themselves.
MSG.22: But God has stood by me, just as he promised, and I'm standing here saying what I've been saying to anyone, whether king or child, who will listen. And everything I'm saying is completely in line with what the prophets and Moses said would happen:
MSG.23: One, the Messiah must die; two, raised from the dead, he would be the first rays of God's daylight shining on people far and near, people both godless and God-fearing."
MSG.24: That was too much for Festus. He interrupted with a shout: "Paul, you're crazy! You've read too many books, spent too much time staring off into space! Get a grip on yourself, get back in the real world!"
MSG.25: But Paul stood his ground. "With all respect, Festus, Your Honor, I'm not crazy. I'm both accurate and sane in what I'm saying.
MSG.26: The King knows what I'm talking about. I'm sure that nothing of what I've said sounds crazy to him. He's known all about it for a long time. You must realize that this wasn't done behind the scenes.
MSG.27: You believe the prophets, don't you, King Agrippa? Don't answer that—I know you believe."
MSG.28: But Agrippa did answer: "Keep this up much longer and you'll make a Christian out of me!"
MSG.29: Paul, still in chains, said, "That's what I'm praying for, whether now or later, and not only you but everyone listening today, to become like me—except, of course, for this prison jewelry!"
MSG.30: The king and the governor, along with Bernice and their advisors, got up
MSG.31: and went into the next room to talk over what they had heard. They quickly agreed on Paul's innocence, saying, "There's nothing in this man deserving prison, let alone death."
MSG.32: Agrippa told Festus, "He could be set free right now if he hadn't requested the hearing before Caesar."

NIV.1:
Then Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.”
So Paul motioned with his hand and began his defense:
NIV.2: “King Agrippa, I consider myself fortunate to stand before you today as I make my defense against all the accusations of the Jews,
NIV.3: and especially so because you are well acquainted with all the Jewish customs and controversies. Therefore, I beg you to listen to me patiently.
NIV.4: “The Jews all know the way I have lived ever since I was a child, from the beginning of my life in my own country, and also in Jerusalem.
NIV.5: They have known me for a long time and can testify, if they are willing, that according to the strictest sect of our religion, I lived as a Pharisee.
NIV.6: And now it is because of my hope in what God has promised our fathers that I am on trial today.
NIV.7: This is the promise our twelve tribes are hoping to see fulfilled as they earnestly serve God day and night. O king, it is because of this hope that the Jews are accusing me.
NIV.8: Why should any of you consider it incredible that God raises the dead?
NIV.9: “I too was convinced that I ought to do all that was possible to oppose the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
NIV.10: And that is just what I did in Jerusalem. On the authority of the chief priests I put many of the saints in prison, and when they were put to death, I cast my vote against them.
NIV.11: Many a time I went from one synagogue to another to have them punished, and I tried to force them to blaspheme. In my obsession against them, I even went to foreign cities to persecute them.
NIV.12: “On one of these journeys I was going to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests.
NIV.13: About noon, O king, as I was on the road, I saw a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, blazing around me and my companions.
NIV.14: We all fell to the ground, and I heard a voice saying to me in Aramaic, ‘Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads.’
NIV.15:
“Then I asked, ‘Who are you, Lord?’
“ ‘I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting,’ the Lord replied.
NIV.16: ‘Now get up and stand on your feet. I have appeared to you to appoint you as a servant and as a witness of what you have seen of me and what I will show you.
NIV.17: I will rescue you from your own people and from the Gentiles. I am sending you to them
NIV.18: to open their eyes and turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan to God, so that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me.’

NIV.20: First to those in Damascus, then to those in Jerusalem and in all Judea, and to the Gentiles also, I preached that they should repent and turn to God and prove their repentance by their deeds.
NIV.21: That is why the Jews seized me in the temple courts and tried to kill me.
NIV.22: But I have had God’s help to this very day, and so I stand here and testify to small and great alike. I am saying nothing beyond what the prophets and Moses said would happen—
NIV.23: that the Christ would suffer and, as the first to rise from the dead, would proclaim light to his own people and to the Gentiles.”
NIV.24: At this point Festus interrupted Paul’s defense. “You are out of your mind, Paul!” he shouted. “Your great learning is driving you insane.”
NIV.25: “I am not insane, most excellent Festus,” Paul replied. “What I am saying is true and reasonable.
NIV.26: The king is familiar with these things, and I can speak freely to him. I am convinced that none of this has escaped his notice, because it was not done in a corner.
NIV.27: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know you do.”
NIV.28: Then Agrippa said to Paul, “Do you think that in such a short time you can persuade me to be a Christian?”
NIV.29: Paul replied, “Short time or long—I pray God that not only you but all who are listening to me today may become what I am, except for these chains.”
NIV.30: The king rose, and with him the governor and Bernice and those sitting with them.
NIV.31: They left the room, and while talking with one another, they said, “This man is not doing anything that deserves death or imprisonment.”
NIV.32: Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar.”

NRSVCE.1: Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.” Then Paul stretched out his hand and began to defend himself:
NRSVCE.2: “I consider myself fortunate that it is before you, King Agrippa, I am to make my defense today against all the accusations of the Jews,
NRSVCE.3: because you are especially familiar with all the customs and controversies of the Jews; therefore I beg of you to listen to me patiently.
NRSVCE.4: “All the Jews know my way of life from my youth, a life spent from the beginning among my own people and in Jerusalem.
NRSVCE.5: They have known for a long time, if they are willing to testify, that I have belonged to the strictest sect of our religion and lived as a Pharisee.
NRSVCE.6: And now I stand here on trial on account of my hope in the promise made by God to our ancestors,
NRSVCE.7: a promise that our twelve tribes hope to attain, as they earnestly worship day and night. It is for this hope, your Excellency, that I am accused by Jews!
NRSVCE.8: Why is it thought incredible by any of you that God raises the dead?
NRSVCE.9: “Indeed, I myself was convinced that I ought to do many things against the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
NRSVCE.10: And that is what I did in Jerusalem; with authority received from the chief priests, I not only locked up many of the saints in prison, but I also cast my vote against them when they were being condemned to death.
NRSVCE.11: By punishing them often in all the synagogues I tried to force them to blaspheme; and since I was so furiously enraged at them, I pursued them even to foreign cities.
NRSVCE.12: “With this in mind, I was traveling to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests,
NRSVCE.13: when at midday along the road, your Excellency, I saw a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining around me and my companions.
NRSVCE.14: When we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It hurts you to kick against the goads.’
NRSVCE.15: I asked, ‘Who are you, Lord?’ The Lord answered, ‘I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.
NRSVCE.16: But get up and stand on your feet; for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you to serve and testify to the things in which you have seen me and to those in which I will appear to you.
NRSVCE.17: I will rescue you from your people and from the Gentiles — to whom I am sending you
NRSVCE.18: to open their eyes so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, so that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me.’
NRSVCE.19: “After that, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision,
NRSVCE.20: but declared first to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout the countryside of Judea, and also to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God and do deeds consistent with repentance.
NRSVCE.21: For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me.
NRSVCE.22: To this day I have had help from God, and so I stand here, testifying to both small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would take place:
NRSVCE.23: that the Messiah must suffer, and that, by being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles.”
NRSVCE.24: While he was making this defense, Festus exclaimed, “You are out of your mind, Paul! Too much learning is driving you insane!”
NRSVCE.25: But Paul said, “I am not out of my mind, most excellent Festus, but I am speaking the sober truth.
NRSVCE.26: Indeed the king knows about these things, and to him I speak freely; for I am certain that none of these things has escaped his notice, for this was not done in a corner.
NRSVCE.27: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe.”
NRSVCE.28: Agrippa said to Paul, “Are you so quickly persuading me to become a Christian?”
NRSVCE.29: Paul replied, “Whether quickly or not, I pray to God that not only you but also all who are listening to me today might become such as I am — except for these chains.”
NRSVCE.30: Then the king got up, and with him the governor and Bernice and those who had been seated with them;
NRSVCE.31: and as they were leaving, they said to one another, “This man is doing nothing to deserve death or imprisonment.”
NRSVCE.32: Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to the emperor.”

NET.1: So Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.” Then Paul held out his hand and began his defense:
NET.2: “Regarding all the things I have been accused of by the Jews, King Agrippa, I consider myself fortunate that I am about to make my defense before you today,
NET.3: because you are especially familiar with all the customs and controversial issues of the Jews. Therefore I ask you to listen to me patiently.
NET.4: Now all the Jews know the way I lived from my youth, spending my life from the beginning among my own people and in Jerusalem.
NET.5: They know, because they have known me from time past, if they are willing to testify, that according to the strictest party of our religion, I lived as a Pharisee.
NET.6: And now I stand here on trial because of my hope in the promise made by God to our ancestors,
NET.7: a promise that our twelve tribes hope to attain as they earnestly serve God night and day. Concerning this hope the Jews are accusing me, Your Majesty!
NET.8: Why do you people think it is unbelievable that God raises the dead?
NET.9: Of course, I myself was convinced that it was necessary to do many things hostile to the name of Jesus the Nazarene.
NET.10: And that is what I did in Jerusalem: Not only did I lock up many of the saints in prisons by the authority I received from the chief priests, but I also cast my vote against them when they were sentenced to death.
NET.11: I punished them often in all the synagogues and tried to force them to blaspheme. Because I was so furiously enraged at them, I went to persecute them even in foreign cities.
NET.12: “While doing this very thing, as I was going to Damascus with authority and complete power from the chief priests,
NET.13: about noon along the road, Your Majesty, I saw a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining everywhere around me and those traveling with me.
NET.14: When we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in Aramaic, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? You are hurting yourself by kicking against the goads.’
NET.15: So I said, ‘Who are you, Lord?’ And the Lord replied, ‘I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.
NET.16: But get up and stand on your feet, for I have appeared to you for this reason, to designate you in advance as a servant and witness to the things you have seen and to the things in which I will appear to you.
NET.17: I will rescue you from your own people and from the Gentiles, to whom I am sending you
NET.18: to open their eyes so that they turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, so that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a share among those who are sanctified by faith in me.’
NET.19: “Therefore, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision,
NET.20: but I declared to those in Damascus first, and then to those in Jerusalem and in all Judea, and to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, performing deeds consistent with repentance.
NET.21: For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple courts and were trying to kill me.
NET.22: I have experienced help from God to this day, and so I stand testifying to both small and great, saying nothing except what the prophets and Moses said was going to happen:
NET.23: that the Christ was to suffer and be the first to rise from the dead, to proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles.”
NET.24: As Paul was saying these things in his defense, Festus exclaimed loudly, “You have lost your mind, Paul! Your great learning is driving you insane!”
NET.25: But Paul replied, “I have not lost my mind, most excellent Festus, but am speaking true and rational words.
NET.26: For the king knows about these things, and I am speaking freely to him, because I cannot believe that any of these things has escaped his notice, for this was not done in a corner.
NET.27: Do you believe the prophets, King Agrippa? I know that you believe.”
NET.28: Agrippa said to Paul, “In such a short time are you persuading me to become a Christian?”
NET.29: Paul replied, “I pray to God that whether in a short or a long time not only you but also all those who are listening to me today could become such as I am, except for these chains.”
NET.30: So the king got up, and with him the governor and Bernice and those sitting with them,
NET.31: and as they were leaving they said to one another, “This man is not doing anything deserving death or imprisonment.”
NET.32: Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar.”
